perturbation.original_text: Attendee shall notify BO MI immediately upon discovery of unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any breach of this Agreement, and shall cooperate with BOMI in every reasonable way to assist BOMI in mitigating the consequences of such use or disclosure and preven ting further unauthorized use or disclosu re. If a third part y brings an action against BOMI arising out of Atte ndee’s breach of this Agreement, BOMI, at its sole discretion, may settle or defend the claim, and Attendee shall pay all settlements, costs, damages and legal fees and expenses arising out of such settlement or defense.
perturbation.changed_text: Attendee shall notify BO MI immediately upon discovery of unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information or any breach of this Agreement, and shall cooperate with BOMI. If a third part y brings an action against BOMI arising out of Atte ndee’s breach of this Agreement, BOMI, at its sole discretion, may settle or defend the claim.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting "in every reasonable way to assist BOMI in mitigating the consequences of such use or disclosure and preven ting further unauthorized use or disclosu re. and Attendee shall pay all settlements, costs, damages and legal fees and expenses arising out of such settlement or defense." shifts the burden of responsibility and financial liability solely on the Attendee, increasing their financial risk. This creates an imbalance and might violate Maryland's consumer protection laws which guard against unfair or deceptive trade practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Maryland Consumer Protection Act, Md. Code Ann., Com. Law § 13-301
perturbation.law_explanation: By placing an unreasonable financial burden and sole responsibility on the Attendee without corresponding protections, the agreement might be deemed an unfair or deceptive trade practice under Maryland's Consumer Protection Act, Md. Code Ann., Com. Law § 13-301.

IN EFFECT Unfair, abusive, or deceptive trade practices include any: (1) False, falsely disparaging, or misleading oral or written statement, visual description, or other representation of any kind which has the capacity, tendency, or effect of deceiving or misleading consumers; (2) Representation that: (i) Consumer goods, consumer realty, or consumer services have a sponsorship, approval, accessory, characteristic, ingredient, use, benefit, or quantity which they do not have; (ii) A merchant has a sponsorship, approval, status, affiliation, or connection which he does not have; (iii) Deteriorated, altered, reconditioned, reclaimed, or secondhand consumer goods are original or new; or (iv) Consumer goods, consumer realty, or consumer services are of a particular standard, quality, grade, style, or model which they are not; (3) Failure to state a material fact if the failure deceives or tends to deceive; (4) Disparagement of the goods, realty, services, or business of another by a false or misleading representation of a material fact; (5) Advertisement or offer of consumer goods, consumer realty, or consumer services: (i) Without intent to sell, lease, or rent them as advertised or offered; or (ii) With intent not to supply reasonably expected public demand, unless the advertisement or offer discloses a limitation of quantity or other qualifying condition; (6) False or misleading representation of fact which concerns: (i) The reason for or the existence or amount of a price reduction; or (ii) A price in comparison to a price of a competitor or to one’s own price at a past or future time; (7) Knowingly false statement that a service, replacement, or repair is needed; (8) False statement which concerns the reason for offering or supplying consumer goods, consumer realty, or consumer services at sale or discount prices; (9) Deception, fraud, false pretense, false premise, misrepresentation, or knowing concealment, suppression, or omission of any material fact with the
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the attendee's obligation to assist BOMI in mitigating the consequences of a breach and removes the attendee's financial responsibility for settlements, costs, damages, and legal fees. The Law Explanation suggests this could be an unfair practice under Maryland's Consumer Protection Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Md. Code Ann., Com. Law § 13–301, defines unfair or deceptive trade practices, including representations that mislead consumers. By removing the financial burden on the attendee arising from their own breach, and shifting it to BOMI, arguably the agreement becomes an unfair or deceptive trade practice because it fails to state a material fact if the failure deceives or tends to deceive.

perturbation.original_text: Attendee understands that BOMI makes no representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information disclosed, and Attendee agrees that neither BOMI nor any of its officers, director s, Attendees, agents, members or attorneys will have any liability to Attendee arising from Attendee’s use of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Attendee understands that BOMI makes no representations or warranties as to the accuracy or completeness of any Confidential Information disclosed.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "and Attendee agrees that neither BOMI nor any of its officers, director s, Attendees, agents, members or attorneys will have any liability to Attendee arising from Attendee’s use of the Confidential Information", the agreement removes a waiver of liability that might otherwise be implied. Maryland law imposes a duty of good faith and fair dealing, and this clause could be interpreted as an attempt to shield BOMI from all liability, even for gross negligence or intentional misconduct. Removing it might be more protective of the Attendee's rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland's Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304 implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Removing the exculpatory clause could potentially expose BOMI to liability if their actions related to the Confidential Information violate this duty.  The original clause sought to waive any such liability, and its absence now creates a potential conflict, by omission, with the UCC's implied covenant.

perturbation.justification: The original text included a waiver of liability for BOMI, whereas the modified text removes this waiver. Maryland law, as evidenced by Scraped Law Snippet 1, imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of every contract within the Maryland Uniform Commercial Code (§ 1-304). By removing the exculpatory clause, the modified contract potentially contradicts this law because BOMI could now be exposed to liability for actions related to the Confidential Information that violate the duty of good faith. The original clause sought to waive such liability, and its absence potentially clashes with the UCC's implied covenant.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will be governed by and c onstrued according to the laws of the State of Maryland (excluding the choice of laws ru les thereof). Attendee hereby consents to the jurisdiction of the courts of the State of Maryland. Any su it brought hereon must be brought in the state or federal courts sitti ng in the State of Maryland, the parties hereto hereby waive any claim or defense that such foru m is not convenient or proper.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will be governed by and c onstrued according to the laws of the State of Maryland (excluding the choice of laws ru les thereof).
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the clauses regarding jurisdiction and venue, the agreement no longer specifies where lawsuits must be filed. Under Maryland law, specifically regarding consumer contracts, there are restrictions on forum selection clauses, especially if they are deemed unfair or inconvenient to the consumer. The omission of this clause removes the Attendee's explicit waiver of objections to Maryland jurisdiction, which might be required for enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Maryland Code, Commercial Law, § 14-1315 places restrictions on forum selection clauses in consumer contracts to protect consumers from unfair venues. While this NDA isn't a typical 'consumer contract', if the testing is seen as providing a service to the Attendee, the lack of a fair forum selection clause, now by omission, could run afoul of the spirit of this law, especially if the Attendee is from out of state and would be unduly burdened by having to litigate in Maryland.

(a) (1) In this section the following words have the meanings indicated. (2) “Consumer contract” means a contract involving the sale, lease, or provision of goods or services which are for personal, family, or household purposes. (3) “Contract”, unless specifically provided otherwise, includes consumer, commercial, and business contracts, covenants, leases of any kind, and tariffs on file with any regulatory authority. (4) (i) “Late fee” means any charge or fee imposed because a payment is not made when the payment is due under the terms of a contract. (ii) “Late fee” includes a fee imposed under subparagraph (i) of this paragraph that is described: 1. As a flat rate; 2. As a percentage of the amount due; or 3. In any other terms. (b) The parties to a contract may agree to require the payment of a late fee when a party fails to make a payment when the payment is due. (c) A contract that requires the payment of a late fee shall disclose, by its terms or by notice: (1) The amount of the late fee; (2) The conditions under which the late fee will be imposed; and (3) The timing for the imposition of the late fee. (d) A late fee imposed under this section is not: (1) Interest; (2) A finance charge; (3) Liquidated damages; or (4) A penalty. (e) This section does not affect a late fee, a finance charge, interest, or any other fee or charge otherwise allowed under applicable law. (f) (1) A late fee included in a consumer contract pursuant to this section is subject to one of the following limitations: (i) 1. The amount of the late fee may be up to $5 per month, or up to 10% per month of the payment amount that is past due, whichever is greater; and 2. No more than 3 monthly late fees may be imposed for any single payment amount that is past due, regardless of the period during which the payment remains past due; or (ii) The amount of the late fee may be up to 1.5% per month of the payment amount that is past due. (2) The amount of the late fee under paragraph (1) of this s
perturbation.justification: The original text specified jurisdiction and venue in Maryland. The changed text omits these clauses. The Law Explanation suggests this omission could be problematic under Maryland law, Commercial Law, § 14-1315, which addresses forum selection clauses in consumer contracts to protect consumers from unfair venues. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'consumer contract' and discusses late fees, but more broadly introduces the context of consumer protection laws in Maryland. While the NDA may not be a typical consumer contract, if the testing is considered a service, the omission might be viewed negatively, potentially contradicting the spirit of consumer protection laws by not ensuring a fair forum for the Attendee.
